questions.question: Explain the concept of exception handling in C++ with a suitable example. Differentiate between throw, try, and catch blocks.
keyPoints: throw is used to raise an exception
try block contains code that might throw
catch block handles the thrown exception
Example showing proper syntax and flow
Exception propagates until caught

questions.question: What is exception slicing? How can it be prevented when catching exceptions in a class hierarchy?
keyPoints: Definition: losing derived class data when caught by value
Occurs when base class catch receives derived exception
Prevention: catch by reference or pointer
Shows polymorphic behavior is lost when sliced
Code example demonstrating the problem and solution

questions.question: Explain the C++ standard exception class hierarchy. List at least four derived exception classes.
keyPoints: std::exception is base class
Two main branches: std::logic_error and std::runtime_error
std::logic_error includes: invalid_argument, length_error, out_of_range
std::runtime_error includes: range_error, overflow_error, runtime_error
Other important: std::bad_alloc, std::bad_cast, std::ios_base::failure

questions.question: What is stack unwinding in C++ exception handling? Explain with an example.
keyPoints: Stack unwinding removes function stack frames
Destructors of local objects are called during unwinding
Ensures proper cleanup before exception is caught
Example showing constructor throws and partial construction cleanup
Mention that throwing in destructor leads to terminate()

questions.question: Differentiate between catching exceptions by value, by reference, and by pointer. Which is recommended and why?
keyPoints: By value: creates copy, causes slicing
By reference: preserves polymorphic behavior, no slicing
By pointer: requires memory management, less common
Recommended: catch by const reference
Reasons: prevents slicing, allows polymorphism, no copy overhead

questions.question: Explain the three levels of exception safety guarantees with examples.
keyPoints: Basic guarantee: invariants broken but no resource leak
Strong guarantee: all-or-nothing, operation either succeeds fully or has no effect
Nothrow guarantee: operation will never throw
Examples for each level from standard library
Real-world scenarios where each applies

questions.question: What is the purpose of the catch(...) block? When and why should it be used?
keyPoints: catch(...) catches all exception types
No access to exception object
Must be last catch block in sequence
Used for cleanup or logging before rethrowing
Should not swallow exceptions without proper handling

questions.question: Design a class hierarchy for custom exceptions with a base class CustomException and two derived classes DatabaseException and NetworkException. Write a C++ program demonstrating proper exception handling in this hierarchy, ensuring that derived class exceptions are caught before base class exceptions.
keyPoints: Define base class CustomException derived from std::exception
Define DatabaseException and NetworkException derived from CustomException
Implement what() method in each class
Demonstrate throw of each exception type
Show proper catch block ordering (derived before base)
Demonstrate catching by reference
Show output for each case

questions.question: Write a C++ program that demonstrates: (a) Rethrowing exceptions, (b) Nested exceptions using std::nested_exception, and (c) Exception specifications using noexcept. Explain the scenarios where each concept is applicable.
keyPoints: Rethrowing: catch block handling partial work then rethrowing
nested_exception: catching one exception while handling another
noexcept: declaring functions that won't throw
Complete code with proper comments
Explanation of when to use each feature
Demonstration of error handling benefits

questions.question: Explain the problem that arises when constructors and destructors throw exceptions. How does C++ handle such situations? Discuss with suitable code examples.
keyPoints: Constructors can throw - already constructed members get destroyed
Destructors should NOT throw - leads to std::terminate()
Stack unwinding handles constructor exceptions
Destructor exception during unwinding calls terminate()
Solution: catch in constructor, never throw in destructor
RAII pattern ensures cleanup even with exceptions
Code examples demonstrating both scenarios

questions.question: Write a C++ program that implements a simple file processing system with proper exception handling. The system should handle different types of errors: file not found (FileNotFoundException), permission denied (PermissionException), and read/write errors (IOException). Use exception hierarchy and demonstrate proper catch ordering.
keyPoints: Create exception class hierarchy with base IOException
FileNotFoundException and PermissionException derived from IOException
FileProcessor class that throws appropriate exceptions
Main function demonstrating each exception type
Proper catch ordering: most specific first
Catching by reference to preserve hierarchy
Proper error messages using what() method
